perturbation.original_text: All such appointments by Agent shall stipulate that MICOA may terminate the subagency at any time without cause upon at least 90 days notice and that the subagency shall comply with all MICOA requirements and duties owed MICOA by Agency concerning solicitation, communications, and service to insureds.
perturbation.changed_text: All such appointments by Agent shall stipulate that MICOA may terminate the subagency at any time without cause upon at least 30 days notice and that the subagency shall comply with all MICOA requirements and duties owed MICOA by Agency concerning solicitation, communications, and service to insureds.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination of a subagency to 30 days may not provide sufficient time for the subagency to wind down its operations, potentially conflicting with general contract law principles of fairness and good faith. Some interpretations might suggest a longer reasonable notice period, especially considering the investment and effort involved in building an agency.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Common Law Principle)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no specific statute, the Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing is a common law principle that requires parties to a contract to act honestly and fairly. Reducing the notice period to 30 days might be viewed as a breach of this covenant if it's deemed unreasonable given the circumstances and the potential harm to the subagency.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 90 days' notice for termination, while the changed text reduces it to 30 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this may conflict with the Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing, especially if the 30-day notice is deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards,' suggesting that a drastically reduced notice period could be viewed as a failure to act in good faith, especially considering the investment and effort involved in building an agency, which aligns with the provided definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The purchase price shall be two times Agency's commissions on business produced directly by Agency during the last 12 full months preceding the termination date. The purchase shall be completed within 60 calendar days after the termination date.
perturbation.changed_text: The purchase price shall be two times Agency's commissions on business produced directly by Agency during the last 12 full months preceding the termination date. The purchase shall be completed within 90 calendar days after the termination date.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the purchase completion timeline to 90 days after termination could present issues regarding the timely transfer of ownership and control of expirations. If MICOA is delaying the payment longer than is reasonably necessary, this could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Common Law Principle)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no specific statute requiring completion in 60 days, extending to 90 days without reasonable justification may violate Nevada's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Unnecessary delay of payment or transferring expirations could be viewed as acting unfairly.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the purchase completion timeline from 60 to 90 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Nevada law if the delay is unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties'. Extending the timeline without justification could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, potentially leading to liability, thus creating a contradiction.
